narration.essentials: Two instructions enter the machine at once — that is what two-wide means.
narration.detailed: This is the wide machine. Where the pipeline fetches one instruction per cycle, this one fetches two: `li a0, 0` and `li t0, 10` are pulled from instruction memory together, in the very first cycle, and travel down the pipe side by side. Watch the fetch stage — two slots, both lit.
narration.expert: Fetch is `issueWidth`-wide: pc and pc+4 index instruction memory in the same cycle, latching an aligned pair. Everything downstream — decode, the register-file read ports, the execute lanes — is replicated to match, so a two-instruction group can advance as a unit. Whether the pair actually ISSUES together is decided later, at issue; fetching two is always free.

narration.essentials: The loop's two arithmetic instructions run in the same cycle, in two execute lanes.
narration.detailed: Here is the payoff, mid-loop. `add a0, a0, t0` and `addi t0, t0, -1` are the loop body's two arithmetic instructions, and the wide machine runs them together: both are in Execute this one cycle, one in each ALU lane. The add pushes the running total to 19; beside it the counter drops to 8. Two instructions, one cycle — the one-wide pipeline would have spent two.
narration.expert: The pair issues because it is independent at issue. `add` reads a0 and t0; `addi` reads t0 and writes t0. That write-after-read on t0 is harmless — both instructions read the register file in the same cycle, so `add` sees the pre-decrement t0 — so there is no intra-pair RAW to refuse them. Two `alu-op` events in one cycle is the signature of a paired EX, and the datapath lights both lanes.

narration.essentials: Same answer — 55 — reached in 44 cycles instead of 56.
narration.detailed: The loop is done: a0 holds 55, the sum 10 + 9 + ... + 1, exactly as on any other machine. Width never changes what a program computes, only how fast. Count the cost: this wide run finished in 44 cycles where the one-wide pipeline needs 56 — an IPC of 0.77 against 0.61. Flip the width toggle back to 1 and watch the 55 stay put while the cycle count climbs to 56.
narration.expert: 34 instructions retire either way — width is a throughput knob, not a correctness one. That is 34 / 44 = 0.77 IPC at width 2 against 34 / 56 = 0.61 at width 1. The speedup is sub-linear because the loop's backward branch mispredicts every pass — the not-taken machine bets fall-through and the branch is taken — and a flush is a flush at any width: the pair cannot fill the shadow the redirect clears. Pairing pays where instructions are independent and the pipe is full, not across a control transfer.
